I believe these are just a few of many predatory, unfair and privacy-infringing practices increasingly taking hold in video game industry.

In recent years, a concept of games as a "live service" has emerged, characterised by a complete reliance to the developer/publisher servers and which are susceptible to unpredictably being phased-out and shut down permanently, without the players retaining an option to play the game after they have paid for it -- in essence, making the video game a perishable commodity with a non-permanent ownership, along with lack of transparency in timing or circumstances of service discontinuation.

paragraphs.text: As a European Union citizen, I wish to express my support for the Digital Fairness Act, and, in parallel, the recent "Stop Killing Games" initiative. I am a lifelong video game enthusiast, having bought countless physical and digital video game products since at least 20 years ago. Nowadays, nearly all of my purchases are digital through several video game vendor platforms across variety of custom and proprietary gaming hardware (such as desktop computers, PlayStation, Nintendo), and I have grown a large collection of video games across all platforms. Over the past 10-15 years, along with many of my peers in this hobby, I have observed a concerning trend of video game developers and publishers employing aggressive, misleading and sometimes outright deceptive game monetisation strategies -- such as gambling-based mechanics designed to entice spending real money, as outlined by the Digital Fairness Act. However, I believe these are just a few of many predatory, unfair and privacy-infringing practices increasingly taking hold in video game industry. In recent years, a concept of games as a "live service" has emerged, characterised by a complete reliance to the developer/publisher servers and which are susceptible to unpredictably being phased-out and shut down permanently, without the players retaining an option to play the game after they have paid for it -- in essence, making the video game a perishable commodity with a non-permanent ownership, along with lack of transparency in timing or circumstances of service discontinuation. This issue is covered by the "Stop Killing Games" initiative, and I believe it should be considered along with this Act. I believe that European Union, already having some of the best customer protection laws in the world, can make a very substantial impact in prevention of increasingly unfair and predatory practices in the global video game industry. Therefore I hope that the Digital Fairness Act leads to increased awareness and legal protection against these issues and that other aforementioned adjacent issues are thoroughly discussed and investigated by the European Commission.
